pet-bottle.title: PET Bottle
pet-bottle.subtitle: Empty, crush slightly, and leave the cap attached.
pet-bottle.commonUses: Water bottles, Juice bottles, Cooking oil containers
steps: Empty the contents: Pour out any remaining liquids or food residue.
Rinse briefly: A quick swirl of water prevents sticky residue from contaminating other materials.
Crush the bottle: Squeeze the air out to save space in your bin and the collection truck.
Leave the cap ON: Screw the cap back on securely. Loose caps fall through sorting grates.
mistakes: Leaving liquid inside: Heavy bottles can be mis-sorted by machines.
Bagging your bottles: Never put recyclables in a plastic trash bag.
Removing the label: You don't need to peel the label off.

faqs.question: What happens after it gets recycled?
faqs.answer: PET bottles are sorted, cleaned, and shredded into tiny flakes to make clothing fibers or new bottles.

faqs.question: What is the environmental impact?
faqs.answer: Recycling a single plastic bottle can save enough energy to power a 60-watt light bulb for up to 6 hours.

styrofoam-cup.title: Styrofoam Cup
styrofoam-cup.subtitle: Cannot be processed in standard curbside bins. Throw in trash.
styrofoam-cup.commonUses: Hot beverage cups, takeout containers, packing peanuts
steps: Check local facilities: Do NOT put in curbside bins. Check if a local specialized facility accepts expanded polystyrene.
Throw in trash: If no local facility exists, it must go in the regular household trash.
mistakes: Putting in curbside bins: It breaks into tiny pieces, contaminating the entire recycling batch and ruining good paper and plastic.

faqs.question: Why is it so hard to recycle?
faqs.answer: It is 95% air, making it very bulky and expensive to transport relative to its actual material value.

hdpe-milk-jug.title: HDPE Milk Jug
hdpe-milk-jug.subtitle: Rinse out all milk residue completely before recycling.
hdpe-milk-jug.commonUses: Milk jugs, juice jugs, heavy-duty cleaning containers
steps: Empty and rinse thoroughly: Milk residue spoils quickly, smells terrible, and contaminates the recycling bin.
Crush to save space: Push the air out so it takes up less room in your bin.
Cap it: Put the plastic cap back on so it gets recycled too.
mistakes: Leaving dairy inside: Rotten dairy creates hazardous conditions for sorting facility workers and attracts pests.

faqs.question: What gets made from recycled HDPE?
faqs.answer: Recycled milk jugs are often turned into durable plastic lumber, picnic tables, and park benches.

plastic-straws.title: Plastic Straws
plastic-straws.subtitle: Too small for sorting machines. Must go in the trash.
plastic-straws.commonUses: Beverages, fast food cups, juice boxes
steps: Throw them in the trash: Plastic straws cannot be recycled curbside.
Opt for reusables: Consider switching to metal, glass, or silicone straws for home use.
mistakes: Wish-cycling: Putting them in the recycling bin hoping they get recycled. They just cause jams or fall through.

faqs.question: Why can't small items be recycled?
faqs.answer: Recycling facilities use large mechanical screens to sort materials. Small items like straws fall right through the gaps and end up as garbage anyway.

pp-food-container.title: PP Food Container
pp-food-container.subtitle: Wash out all grease and leftover food before binning.
pp-food-container.commonUses: Takeout boxes, Tupperware, meal prep containers
steps: Scrape out food: Use a spatula to get all solid food out.
Wash with soap: Ensure all oil and grease are removed.
Dry it: Shake out excess water before putting it in the bin.
mistakes: Leaving sauce inside: Grease ruins the plastic melting process and can contaminate surrounding paper in the collection truck.

faqs.question: Can I recycle black plastic food containers?
faqs.answer: Usually no. The optical sorting lasers at recycling plants cannot 'see' black plastic against the black conveyor belts, so it gets sorted as trash.

pvc-pipe.title: PVC Pipe
pvc-pipe.subtitle: Requires specialized drop-off facilities or hardware stores.
steps: Do not put in curbside bins: PVC requires a completely different melting point than standard household plastics.
Find a drop-off: Check local hardware stores or municipal construction waste centers.
Donate: If the pipe is clean and usable, donate it to a local Habitat for Humanity ReStore.
mistakes: Mixing with PET: Even a tiny amount of PVC can ruin an entire batch of recycled PET plastic by burning and releasing toxic gas.

faqs.question: Why is PVC so problematic?
faqs.answer: PVC contains chlorine. When melted with other plastics, it creates hazardous acids that ruin the batch and damage machinery.

bubble-wrap.title: Bubble Wrap
bubble-wrap.subtitle: Do not put in curbside bins. Take to grocery store drop-offs.
steps: Pop or deflate: Flatten it out to save space.
Take to a store: Drop it off at the 'Plastic Film' collection bins located at the entrance of most major grocery stores.
mistakes: Putting it in curbside bins: Thin film plastics wrap around the spinning gears of recycling sorting machines, causing massive plant shutdowns.

faqs.question: Can I recycle it if the bubbles are popped?
faqs.answer: No. The issue is the thin, stretchy nature of the film, not the air bubbles. It must always go to store drop-offs.

yogurt-cup.title: Yogurt Cup
yogurt-cup.subtitle: Rinse clean. Foil lids must go in the trash bin separately.
steps: Remove the foil lid: The lid is often a mix of foil and plastic and must go in the trash.
Rinse the cup: Make sure no dairy is left inside.
Recycle the cup: Place the clean plastic cup in your bin.
mistakes: Leaving the foil lid on: Mixed materials confuse the sorting machines and lower the quality of the recycled plastic.

faqs.question: Do I need to run it through the dishwasher?
faqs.answer: No, a quick but thorough rinse in the sink is perfectly fine!

plastic-cutlery.title: Plastic Cutlery
plastic-cutlery.subtitle: Cannot be sorted due to size and material. Throw in trash.
steps: Wash and reuse: If they are durable enough, wash them and reuse them for future picnics.
Throw in trash: If broken or unwanted, they must go in the garbage.
mistakes: Putting them in recycling: Their odd shapes and small profiles mean they jam machinery or fall through the cracks.

faqs.question: What if they are labeled 'compostable'?
faqs.answer: Compostable cutlery must go to an industrial composting facility. Do NOT put compostable plastics in your standard recycling bin.

plastic-jug.title: Plastic Jug
plastic-jug.subtitle: Empty liquid, rinse thoroughly, and keep the cap screwed on.
plastic-jug.commonUses: Laundry detergent, fabric softener, heavy household cleaners
steps: Empty completely: Ensure no soap or chemicals remain inside.
Rinse it out: Add a little water, shake it, and pour it out.
Replace cap: Leave the heavy plastic cap screwed securely back on.
mistakes: Leaving soap inside: Detergent can leak onto paper and cardboard in the recycling truck, rendering the paper un-recyclable.

faqs.question: Can I recycle jugs that held motor oil?
faqs.answer: No. Plastic that held motor oil, antifreeze, or hazardous chemicals must be treated as hazardous waste.

plastic-tub.title: Plastic Tub
plastic-tub.subtitle: Usually accepted, but must be completely clean and dry.
plastic-tub.commonUses: Butter, sour cream, cream cheese containers
steps: Clean it: Wash the tub with soap to remove all grease and dairy.
Dry it: Shake it out.
Recycle tub and lid: Both the tub and the plastic lid are usually recyclable. Leave the lid attached.
mistakes: Nesting them together: Don't stack the tubs tightly inside one another. Keep them loose so the scanners can read them.

faqs.question: Can I recycle it if the print is directly on the plastic?
faqs.answer: Yes! The ink is burned off or separated during the high-heat recycling process.

plastic-spoons.title: Plastic Spoons
plastic-spoons.subtitle: Too small for sorting machinery. Please put in regular trash.
steps: Throw in trash: Just like forks and knives, spoons cannot be processed curbside.
Request 'No Utensils': When ordering food delivery, check the box asking them not to include plastic cutlery to prevent waste.
mistakes: Assuming all plastic is recyclable: Shape and size matter just as much as the resin code. Spoons are simply too small.

faqs.question: Why are some plastics just not recyclable?
faqs.answer: Recycling is a business. If it costs more money to sort, clean, and melt a cheap plastic spoon than the resulting material is worth, facilities won't process it.

cardboard-box.title: Cardboard Box
cardboard-box.subtitle: Flatten completely, remove packing peanuts, and keep dry.
cardboard-box.commonUses: Shipping boxes, moving boxes, appliance packaging
steps: Empty it out: Ensure no packing peanuts, foam, or plastic film remains inside.
Flatten completely: Break the box down flat to save space in bins and collection trucks.
Keep it dry: Wet cardboard loses its structural integrity and may be rejected.
mistakes: Leaving it unflattened: Taking up too much volume causes recycling trucks to fill up faster, increasing emissions.
Getting it wet: Moldy or soggy cardboard cannot be pulped properly.

faqs.question: Do I need to remove all the tape?
faqs.answer: Most modern recycling facilities can handle some tape, but it is best to pull off large strips of plastic packing tape if possible.

pizza-box.title: Greasy Pizza Box
pizza-box.subtitle: Grease ruins paper fibers. Throw the greasy half in the trash.
pizza-box.commonUses: Takeout pizza, oily baked goods delivery
steps: Tear off the lid: If the top half of the box is clean and grease-free, tear it off and recycle it.
Trash the bottom: Throw the greasy, cheese-stained bottom half into the regular trash or a municipal compost bin (if accepted locally).
mistakes: Recycling the whole box: A heavily greased pizza box can contaminate an entire bale of clean paper, causing it to be sent to a landfill.

faqs.question: Why is grease so bad for paper recycling?
faqs.answer: Paper recycling involves mixing the paper with water to make a slurry. Since oil and water don't mix, the grease separates and creates oily spots, ruining the newly formed paper.

glass-bottle.title: Glass Bottle
glass-bottle.subtitle: Empty contents and rinse. Glass can be recycled infinitely.
glass-bottle.commonUses: Wine bottles, beer bottles, pasta sauce jars
steps: Empty and rinse: Make sure no liquid or food residue is left inside.
Remove caps/lids: Metal or plastic caps should be removed and sorted into their respective bins.
Place gently in bin: Try not to break the glass when dropping it in your recycling bin.
mistakes: Breaking it intentionally: Broken glass is dangerous for sanitation workers and makes sorting by color much more difficult.

faqs.question: Do I need to peel off the paper labels?
faqs.answer: No! The labels are burned off easily during the high-heat melting process.

window-glass.title: Broken Window Glass
window-glass.subtitle: Treated window glass melts differently than bottles. Do not bin.
window-glass.commonUses: House windows, mirrors, drinking glasses, Pyrex
steps: Handle safely: Sweep up broken pieces carefully.
Wrap it up: Wrap the shards in heavy newspaper or put them in a sealed cardboard box.
Label and trash: Label the box 'Broken Glass' and place it in the regular household trash.
mistakes: Mixing it with bottle glass: Do not put window glass, mirrors, or lightbulbs in the glass recycling bin.

faqs.question: Why can't it be recycled with regular bottles?
faqs.answer: Window glass and mirrors are treated with chemical additives to make them durable. They have a different melting point than container glass, and mixing them will ruin a newly recycled batch.

aluminum-can.title: Aluminum Can
aluminum-can.subtitle: Do not crush. Rinse lightly and place directly in the bin.
aluminum-can.commonUses: Soda cans, sparkling water, beer cans
steps: Empty contents: Pour out any remaining sugary liquid to avoid attracting bees and pests.
Do NOT crush: Keep the can in its 3D shape unless your specific local rules tell you otherwise.
Toss in bin: Place the whole can directly into your curbside recycling.
mistakes: Crushing the cans: Many modern sorting facilities use optical scanners. If you crush an aluminum can flat, the machine might mistake it for paper and sort it incorrectly.

faqs.question: How efficient is aluminum recycling?
faqs.answer: Incredibly efficient! Recycling an aluminum can saves 95% of the energy required to make a new can from raw bauxite ore.

scrap-metal.title: Scrap Metal & Nails
scrap-metal.subtitle: Too sharp and small for sorting machines. Take to a scrap yard.
scrap-metal.commonUses: Construction debris, broken tools, loose hardware
steps: Collect safely: Store sharp nails, screws, and broken metal tools in a dedicated container.
Take to a scrap yard: Local scrap metal dealers will often take these items (and sometimes pay you for them).
Do NOT bin: Never put loose scrap metal in your curbside recycling bin.
mistakes: Throwing loose nails in the recycling bin: Sharp metal pieces can puncture the tires of collection trucks and severely injure workers.

faqs.question: Are small items like paperclips recyclable?
faqs.answer: Usually no. They are too small and will fall straight through the gaps in the sorting screens, ending up as landfill waste.

smartphone.title: Old Smartphone
smartphone.subtitle: Take to specialized e-waste drop-off. Wipe your data first.
steps: Back up data: Save your photos and contacts to the cloud or a computer.
Factory Reset: Completely wipe the device to protect your privacy.
Remove cards: Take out any SIM cards or external MicroSD cards.
Drop off safely: Take the phone to a certified e-waste recycling center or retail store drop-box.
mistakes: Throwing it in the regular trash: Phones contain toxic heavy metals like lead and mercury that will leach into the soil and groundwater if sent to a landfill.

faqs.question: What materials are actually recovered from phones?
faqs.answer: E-waste recyclers extract precious metals like gold, silver, copper, and rare earth elements so they can be reused in new electronics.

swollen-battery.title: Swollen Battery
swollen-battery.subtitle: Severe fire hazard! Do not bin. Take to hazardous waste facility.
swollen-battery.commonUses: Damaged phones, old laptops, broken power banks
steps: Do NOT puncture: Never squish, poke, or attempt to deflate a swollen battery.
Isolate it: If actively expanding or hot, place it in a fireproof container or a bucket of sand.
Dispose immediately: Take it directly to a municipal Household Hazardous Waste (HHW) facility.
mistakes: Putting it in ANY household bin: Placing a lithium-ion battery in the garbage or recycling is extremely dangerous. When the truck compacts the waste, the battery will crush and ignite, causing a massive fire.

faqs.question: Why do these batteries swell up?
faqs.answer: Swelling occurs when the internal cells degrade due to age, overheating, or overcharging. This causes a toxic, flammable gas to build up inside the casing.
